title: FINS3640 Lecture3 v2
body:
The Use of Duration and Convexity in Bonds
Trading and Hedging
Lectures 3
 Course Outline

 Review of the basics of bond pricing
     Discounted cash flows
     Relationships of bond price and YTM

 Measuring duration and convexity
     Calculation
     Properties and interpretation

 Applications of the use of duration and convexity
     Portfolio hedging
     Portfolio replication: Indexing
     Balance sheet immunization
     Portfolio immunization: Immunization of cash-flows




                                                FINS 3640
                                              Pricing of bonds
• A bond is a debt security for which the issuer (the borrower) promises 1) to pay periodic interest
  (coupons) and 2) repay the borrowed amount at maturity


• Fixed income securities promise fixed cash flows and are therefore relatively easy to price.
     • In particular for bonds without any special options - called « plain vanilla »
     • We just need to discount the future expected cash flows

• Ingredients of bond pricing:
    • Discount rate per period:
         • Either we can use the constant Yield-to-maturity of the bond for all cash-flows (y0 )
         • Or the yield-curve - the maturity specifics discount rate (r0,𝑡𝑡 ) for each cash-flow received at time t.
    • Maturity: T (total number of periods)
    • There are two types of cash flows:
         • Coupon (or interest payments): C
              • C is defined as FV * CR where CR is the coupon rate. CR is always expressed on an annual nominal basis. It will need to be
                expressed per period if the payment are not annual (i.e., divide CR by 2 to get the semi-annual coupon rate).
         • The nominal or face value received at maturity: NV or FV

        Application 3: Price-Yield relationship and investment decisions
              How can we make use of all these relationships for Investment decisions?


  To answer the questions below, suppose that we are a bond portfolio manager, and we cannot
  take short position in bonds but only tilts our portfolio toward bonds with certain
  characteristics (maturity, coupon rate, YTM, etc…)


• Question 1: If we anticipate an increase in interest rates (thus decrease in prices), to minimize our
  exposure to this change, we should tilt our portfolio toward bonds with which characteristics?
• Question 2: If we anticipate a decrease in interest rates (hence higher prices), to maximize our gains
  from these variations, we should tilt our portfolio toward bonds with which characteristics?




                                                FINS 3640                                             11
        Application 3: Price-Yield Relationship and investment decisions
  How can we make use of all these relationships for Investment decisions? To answer the questions
  below, suppose that we are a bond portfolio manager, and we cannot take short position in bonds.


• Question 1: If we anticipate an increase in interest rates (thus decrease in prices), to minimize our
  exposure to this change, we should tilt our portfolio toward bonds with which characteristics?
• Question 2: If we anticipate a decrease in interest rates (hence higher prices), to maximize our gains from
  these variations, we should tilt our portfolio toward bonds with which characteristics?

• Answer to Question 1: We should tilt our portfolio towards bonds with
    • short maturity (rule 3)
    • High YTM (rule 4)
    • high coupon rates (rule 5)
• Answer to Question 2: We should tilt our portfolio towards bonds with
    • long maturity
    • low YTM
    • low coupon rates

                                                 FINS 3640                                                12
                                              Macaulay Duration

• Bond fund managers, arbitragers and traders need a measure of sensitivity of the prices relative to
  interest rate changes to establish hedging and trading strategies.
• Macaulay developed a method for assessing the sensitivity of a bond, which captures the effective
  maturity of bonds
Duration (denoted D)
   • Is the weighted average maturity of various payments (cash flows) of the bond.
   • Each weight is proportional to its relative importance in the total price.

                                                       𝐶𝐶𝐹𝐹𝑡𝑡 /(1+𝑦𝑦0 )𝑡𝑡
                 𝐷𝐷0 = ∑𝑇𝑇𝑡𝑡=1 𝑡𝑡 × 𝑤𝑤𝑡𝑡 with 𝑤𝑤𝑡𝑡 =                      where 𝐶𝐶𝐹𝐹𝑡𝑡 is the cash-flow at time t
                                                                𝑃𝑃0


     • When the bond pays coupons semi-annually, the duration is calculated using semi-annual
       quantities for 𝐶𝐶𝐹𝐹𝑡𝑡 , 𝑦𝑦, and t (just like when applying the bond pricing formula). In this case, the
       duration obtained is semi-annual, 𝐷𝐷0,𝑠𝑠 , and needs to be divided by 2 to get the annual duration
       (𝐷𝐷0,𝑎𝑎 = 𝐷𝐷0,𝑠𝑠 /2).

                                      Macaulay Duration
Computing the duration with your calculator:

   The duration for a coupon-paying bond is

                                 𝐷𝐷0 =           −
                                           𝑦𝑦0     𝑐𝑐 1 + 𝑦𝑦0 𝑇𝑇 − 1 + 𝑦𝑦0


with T the total number of periods, y the yield-to-maturity, and c the coupon rate.

 When calculating the duration of a semi-annual coupon bond, you must work in semesters to
  obtain 𝐷𝐷0,𝑠𝑠 and then divide by 2 to get 𝐷𝐷0,𝑎𝑎 .

Note
The formula only applies if we are at the time of payment of the coupon

 *This approximation is exact only if all bonds have the same YTM

17                                                                                   FINS 3640                                 17
                                              Properties of duration
• The price-YTM relationships we have seen show the link between bond prices and some bond characteristics.
    • Duration allows to quantify those relationships
• If you want to bet on interest rates, the duration of your position indicates how aggressive you are
• If you want to track a bond index, the duration allows you to replicate the sensitivity of the index.
• Duration depends on maturity, coupon and YTM.


Properties:
• The longer the duration, the more sensitive the price is to interest
     rate changes
• The duration of a coupon bond is always smaller than its maturity
• The duration of a zero-coupon bond is equal to its maturity
• The duration is inversely related to the YTM
• The duration is inversely related to the coupon rate


18                                                        FINS 3640                                           18
                                  Applications of duration

 Indexing
  Track the performance and the risk of a bond index. Also called passive management, or index
     management
 Portfolio hedging
  Protect the value of a bond portfolio by taking a short position in a hedging instrument (e.g.
     another bond or Bond ETF)
 Balance sheet immunization (Gap management)
  The goal is to reduce the gap in duration between Assets and Liabilities interest rate exposure to
     protect equity value.
 Portfolio Immunization
  Make sure a certain amount will be available in the future: Amount received in the future is
     independent of interest rates

19                                            FINS 3640                                             19
                                     The theory of bond portfolio hedging
• Let us suppose we are a bond portfolio manager and we want to hedge interest rate risk exposure of our portfolio that we
  denote Pt .


• To do this, we need to take another position in a bond or a bond ETF (P1,t ) in such a way so that the combined position
                                                                    ∗
  (original portfolio + new position) has zero modified duration: 𝐷𝐷𝑐𝑐,𝑡𝑡 =0.
     • The goal is to ensure that any losses on the bond portfolio are offset by the gains on the hedging position.
     • One must continually rebalance the hedge because duration changes with time and interest rates.


                                  𝛥𝛥𝑃𝑃    𝐶𝐶
                      ∗                              ∗
•    Why? Well, if 𝐷𝐷𝑐𝑐,𝑡𝑡 =0 then 𝐶𝐶𝑡𝑡        = −𝐷𝐷𝑐𝑐,𝑡𝑡 × Δyt = −0 × Δyt = 0 (i.e., no interest rate risk).
                                   𝑃𝑃𝑡𝑡

                                                                   𝐷𝐷1,𝑡𝑡 𝑝𝑝,𝑡𝑡                (1−w1,t )P1,t

20                                                                              FINS 3640                                                20
                                   Balance sheet immunization: Context
• Balance sheet immunization is a special version of price hedging
• Balance sheet immunization is particularly useful for banks and insurance companies:
• Let us first consider banks:
     • The long position corresponds to assets (eg: bank loans, mortgages, etc…)
     • The short position is composed of its liabilities (eg: customers deposits, checking account, etc…)
     • The problem is that normally, the duration of assets is much longer than that of liabilities
            • So if interest rates rise, the value of assets decreases more than the value of liabilities. Problem?
            • The fact that assets are more exposed to interest rate risk implies that the value of equity will decrease if interest
               rate rises!


• Let us consider insurances or pension funds:
     • The asset side corresponds to pensioners'’ contributions or insurance premia (eg: short maturity)
     • The liabilities (eg: life insurance payments, benefit obligations (retiree payments) etc…)
     • The problem is that normally, the duration of assets is much smaller than that of liabilities
           • So if interest rates decreases, the value of assets increases less than the value of liabilities. Problem?
           • The fact that liabilities are more exposed to interest rate risk implies that the value of equity will decrease if interest
             rate decreases!

  21                                                          FINS 3640                                                            21
                                  Balance sheet immunization: Equity Exposure
                                                                   Balance sheet
                                                      Assets                       Liabilities


                                                                                   Equities


• From the accounting equality, we know that Asset (𝑃𝑃𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 ) = Liability (𝑃𝑃𝐿𝐿,𝑡𝑡 ) + Equity (𝑃𝑃𝐸𝐸,𝑡𝑡 )
• Thus, 𝑃𝑃𝐸𝐸,𝑡𝑡 = 𝑃𝑃𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 – 𝑃𝑃𝐿𝐿,𝑡𝑡
• And, Δ𝑃𝑃𝐸𝐸,𝑡𝑡 = Δ𝑃𝑃𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 − Δ𝑃𝑃𝐿𝐿,𝑡𝑡


• By using the modified duration approximation for asset and liabilities, we get:
                 ∗                           ∗
• Δ𝑃𝑃𝐸𝐸,𝑡𝑡 = −𝐷𝐷𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 × 𝑃𝑃𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 × Δ𝑦𝑦𝑡𝑡 − −𝐷𝐷𝐿𝐿,𝑡𝑡 × 𝑃𝑃𝐿𝐿,𝑡𝑡 × Δ𝑦𝑦𝑡𝑡
                    ∗                     𝑷𝑷

                                Balance sheet immunization: Banks

• The difference in the asset and liabilities price movements is called the duration gap:

                                                ∗            ∗                    𝑷𝑷𝑳𝑳,𝒕𝒕
                                               D𝑮𝑮𝑮𝑮𝑮𝑮,𝒕𝒕 = D𝑨𝑨,𝒕𝒕 − 𝑫𝑫∗𝑳𝑳,𝒕𝒕 ×
                                                                                  𝑷𝑷𝑨𝑨,𝒕𝒕
⇒The idea is to minimize the duration gap
• The gap is managed by adjusting the assets and/or liabilities:
    • Reducing the duration of assets (variable rate mortgages, etc.) and/or
    • Increase the duration of liabilities (non-redeemable certificates of deposit, etc.)


• What to do to change the duration of assets and liabilities for banks:
• Asset: Interest rate swaps, reduce the term of mortgages issued, increase the mortgages with floating interest
  rates…
• Liabilities: Increase the demand for term deposit, issue long-term debt…




25                                                        FINS 3640                                                25
                                           Portfolio immunization
• Changes in interest rates affect the total yield of a bond in 2 ways:
    • Price effect: Impact on resale prices
         • Changes in interest rates implies that bond value changes with the level of interest rates.
    • Income effect: Impact on the future value (FV) of coupons
         • Changes in interest rates imply that coupons received will be reinvested with the prevalent level of
            interest rates.
    ⇒ These two effects are in opposite directions
    ⇒If interest rises, the bond value when sold will be less but the value of all the coupons reinvested will be higher


• If investment horizon = duration, these two effects offset and the portfolio is immunized
• If investment horizon>duration, Income effect>price effect
      • Good if the rates are anticipated to increase
• If investment horizon<duration, Income effect<price effect
      • Good if the rates are anticipated to decrease


• Let us illustrate these two effects numerically.

                                                       FINS 3640                                         26
                                                                                                                     26
                                    Portfolio immunization

                                               FINS 3640                                             27
                                                                                                            27
                                           Portfolio immunization
• The reason for this difference is the duration:
    • For the 5-year bond: D = 4.31
     • For the 6-year bond: D = 4.99 which is almost equal to 5 our investment horizon.


• So when D equals the horizon, the realized return on the bond investment is immune to small changes in interest
  rates.


• Does this mean that we can ensure a specific yield forever?
• No, there are some restrictions:
     • First, one can only "fix" a return in line with current available rates (8% in the example)
     • Then gradually as time passes, we need to rebalance the portfolio to match the new horizon (decrease) with the
       new duration (also decrease)
     • If the strategy is properly implemented, the realized yield will be very close to the "fixed" or immune yield

29                                                                        FINS 3640                                                              29
                              Application 6: Portfolio immunization


A private wealth portfolio manager has a client that plan on purchasing an apartment in Bondi in 2 years from today. The
client want to have 1 000 000 $ cash down for the deposit and ask the portfolio manager to accommodate his need. The
portfolio manager can trade two bonds that pay coupons annually.

   • Bond 1 : T= 3 years, CR=8%, YTM = 10%, annual payments.
   • Bond 2 : T= 1 year, CR=7%, YTM = 10%, annual payments.

Q1. How could the portfolio manager make sure to have 1 000 000 $ in two years for his client? Help him designing the
right strategy.

Q2. Illustrate the performance of your strategy if the YTM of the two bonds in one year from now were: 9% (Case 1),
unchanged (Case 2), or 11% (Case 3).
